title: Charmed Life - Chapter 5 (Fragment 2)
text:
вАЬI donвАЩt believe it!вАЭ Gwendolen whispered to Cat. вАЬIt must be an illusion. TheyвАЩre trying to make me feel small.вАЭ

They went out and looked after lunch. They had to be fairly cautious about it because Mr. Saunders was taking his afternoon off in a deck chair under one of the cedars, reading a yellow paperback book which seemed to amuse him a great deal. Gwendolen sauntered out into the middle of the lawn and pretended to be admiring the Castle. She pretended to tie her bootlace and prodded the turf with her fingers.

вАЬI donвАЩt understand it!вАЭ she said. Being a witch, she knew the close, smooth turf was no illusion. вАЬIt really is all right! How was it done?вАЭ

вАЬThey must have carted in new turf while we were having lessons,вАЭ Cat suggested.

вАЬDonвАЩt be stupid!вАЭ said Gwendolen. вАЬNew turf would all be in squares still, and this isnвАЩt.вАЭ

Mr. Saunders called to them.

Gwendolen looked, for a second, more apprehensive than Cat had ever seen her. But she hid it fairly well and led the way casually over to the deck chair. Cat saw that the yellow book was in French. Fancy being able to laugh at something in French! Mr. Saunders must be a learned magician as well as a strong one.

Mr. Saunders laid the book facedown on the once-more-beautiful grass and smiled up at them. вАЬYou two went away so quickly that you never gave me time to dish you out your pocket money. Here you are.вАЭ He handed them each a large silver coin. Cat stared at his. It was a crown pieceвАФfive whole shillings. He had never had so much money to spend in his life. Mr. Saunders added to his amazement by saying, вАЬYouвАЩll get that every Wednesday. I donвАЩt know whether youвАЩre savers or spenders. What Julia and Roger usually do is to go down to the village and blow it all on sweets.вАЭ

вАЬThank you,вАЭ said Cat, вАЬvery much. Shall we go down to the village, Gwendolen?вАЭ

вАЬWe may as well,вАЭ Gwendolen agreed. She was divided between a defiant desire to stay at the Castle and face whatever trouble was coming over the moles and relief at an excuse to get away. вАЬI expect Chrestomanci will send for me as soon as he realizes it was me,вАЭ she said as they walked down the avenue of trees.

вАЬDo you think it was Mr. Saunders who put the lawn right?вАЭ Cat asked.

Gwendolen frowned. вАЬHe couldnвАЩt have. He was teaching us.вАЭ

вАЬThose gardeners,вАЭ suggested Cat. вАЬSome of them could be warlocks. They did turn up awfully quickly to forbid us things.вАЭ

Gwendolen laughed scornfully. вАЬThink of the Willing Warlock.вАЭ

Cat did, a little dubiously. The Willing Warlock was not much more gifted than Mrs. Sharp. He was usually hired for heavy carrying jobs, or to make the wrong horse win at the races. вАЬAll the same,вАЭ he argued, вАЬthey could be specialistsвАФgarden warlocks.вАЭ

Gwendolen only laughed again.

The village was just beyond the Castle gates, at the foot of the hill where the Castle stood. It was a pretty place, around a big green. Across the green, there were shops: a beautiful bow-fronted bakerвАЩs and an equally beautiful sweet shop and post office. Cat wanted to visit both, but Gwendolen stopped at a third shop, which was a junk shop. Cat did not mind going into that either. It looked interesting. But Gwendolen shook her head irritably and stopped a village boy who was loitering near it.

вАЬI was told a Mr. Baslam lives in this village. Can you tell me where he lives?вАЭ

The boy made a face. вАЬHim? HeвАЩs no good. Down there, at the end of that alley, if you really want to know.вАЭ And he stood looking at them, with the air of someone who has earned sixpence for his pains.

vocabulary.definition: Showing respect and warm approval.
vocabulary.example: She pretended to be admiring the Castle.

vocabulary.definition: A narrow passage or lane between or behind buildings.
vocabulary.example: At the end of that alley.

vocabulary.definition: To surprise someone greatly.
Anxious or fearful that something bad or unpleasant will happen.
A straight path or road lined with trees on both sides.
To spend money carelessly or extravagantly.
In a relaxed and informal way.
Being careful regarding something to avoid harm.
Careful to avoid potential problems or dangers.
vocabulary.example: They had to be fairly cautious about it.

vocabulary.definition: A folding chair with a fabric seat for relaxing outdoors.
Showing resistance or disobedience.
In a doubtful or questionable manner.
To make a facial expression showing disapproval or concentration.
Having exceptional talent or natural ability.
Something that appears to be real but is false or deceptive.
A shop selling second-hand or old items of little value.
To stand or wait around without apparent purpose.
Small amount of money given regularly to a child by their parents.
To act as if something is true when it is not.
vocabulary.example: She pretended to tie her bootlace.

vocabulary.definition: Feeling of reassurance and comfort after stress or worry.
vocabulary.example: Relief at an excuse to get away.

vocabulary.definition: To walk in a slow, relaxed manner, without hurry or effort.
vocabulary.example: Gwendolen sauntered out into the middle of the lawn.

vocabulary.definition: Using irony to mock or convey contempt.
In a way that shows disdain or contempt.
A person who concentrates primarily on a particular subject or activity.
